features.summary: Caterpillar reported Q1 2025 sales of $14.2 billion, down 10% year-over-year, with adjusted operating profit margin of 18.3%. The company achieved record organic backlog growth of $5 billion to $35 billion total, driven by strong order rates across all segments. Machine sales to users were better than expected, declining only 1% versus prior year. Energy & Transportation sales to users grew 13%, primarily from power generation demand for data centers. The company deployed $4.3 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. Joe Creed will succeed Jim Umpleby as CEO on May 1st.
features.management_outlook: Management expects Q2 sales similar to prior year, with Energy & Transportation growth offsetting declines in Construction Industries and Resource Industries. For full year 2025, in a pre-tariff scenario, sales are expected to be about flat versus 2024 with margins in the top half of target range. Even in an alternative scenario with negative economic growth in the second half, sales would only be down slightly versus 2024. The company remains confident in staying within target margin and free cash flow ranges despite current uncertainties.
features.risks: Tariffs represent the primary near-term risk, with an estimated $250-350 million cost headwind in Q2 before mitigation efforts. The fluid tariff situation creates uncertainty around pricing and supply chain decisions. Potential negative economic growth in the second half of 2025 could impact demand, particularly in Construction Industries. Price realization remains unfavorable year-over-year, especially in the first half of 2025. Currency impacts and competitive pressures in various regions also present ongoing challenges.
features.key_drivers: Record backlog of $35 billion provides strong visibility and supports sales resilience across diverse end markets. Power generation demand, particularly from data centers requiring reciprocating engines, drives significant growth in Energy & Transportation. Merchandising programs implemented in 2024 continue yielding positive results with better-than-expected machine sales to users. The company's global manufacturing footprint and strong balance sheet position it well to navigate tariff impacts through various mitigation strategies including supply chain adjustments and cost controls.
